title: Sliding Window
description: Two Pointers' same-direction cousin: sliding a window across an array, updating its sum incrementally instead of recomputing it from scratch.
objectives: Understand the problem sliding window solves
Solve the fixed-size max-subarray-sum problem in O(n)

sections.text: Two Pointers previewed a same-direction variant. Sliding window is exactly that idea, specialized for tracking a contiguous "window" of elements as it moves across an array.
2. The Problem: Recomputing Every Window Sum
The inner loop redoes k additions for every single window position, even though consecutive windows overlap almost entirely — this is O(n × k), wasteful repeated work.
Sliding window maintains a running result over a contiguous range of elements, updating it incrementally as the range moves by one position, instead of recomputing it from scratch.
4. The Key Insight: Subtract Leaving, Add Entering

sections.code:
// Moving the window one step right:
windowSum = windowSum - numbers[i] + numbers[i + k]; // remove the element leaving, add the one entering

sections.text: Two consecutive windows of size k share k-1 elements — only one element leaves (the old start) and one enters (the new end). Updating the sum by subtracting the leaving element and adding the entering one avoids re-adding everything in between.
6. Complexity: O(n) Instead of O(n × k)
Each slide does two operations (one subtract, one add), regardless of k — the total work across the whole array is O(n), a real improvement over the naive O(n × k), especially when k is large.

This is only a preview — some problems need the window's size to grow and shrink dynamically (for example, "find the shortest subarray with sum ≥ target"), rather than staying fixed at k. That's a variable-size sliding window, built from the same core idea but with both ends moving independently as needed.

sections.title: A 3-day moving average, updated daily
sections.text: Tracking a rolling 3-day average temperature doesn't require re-adding all three days from scratch every single day — you drop yesterday's oldest day from the total and add today's new reading. That's a sliding window: the total updates incrementally as the window of days shifts forward by one.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Recomputing the window sum from scratch on every slide — defeats the entire purpose; use subtract-leaving/add-entering instead.
Getting the leaving index wrong — the element leaving the window at step i is numbers[i - k], not numbers[i].
Forgetting to initialize the first window's sum with a normal loop before starting the sliding updates.
Applying a fixed-size window approach to a problem that actually needs a variable-size window, or vice versa.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What problem does the sliding window technique solve?
questions.answer: Avoiding repeated, redundant work when computing a running result (like a sum) over every contiguous range of a fixed size in an array.

questions.question: How is a fixed-size window's sum updated when it slides by one position?
questions.answer: Subtract the element leaving the window (the old start) and add the element entering it (the new end) — windowSum = windowSum - numbers[i-k] + numbers[i].

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the sliding window approach for finding the maximum sum subarray of size k, compared to the naive approach?
questions.answer: O(n) with sliding window, versus O(n × k) for naively recomputing each window's sum from scratch.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Sliding window maintains a running result, like a sum, over a contiguous range of elements, updating it incrementally as the window moves rather than recomputing it from scratch each time. For a fixed-size window of length k, sliding it by one position only requires subtracting the element that just left and adding the element that just entered — windowSum - numbers[i-k] + numbers[i] — turning an O(n × k) naive approach into O(n). This is a close cousin of the same-direction variant of Two Pointers, and can also be extended to variable-size windows for problems where the window itself needs to grow and shrink dynamically.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Sliding Window
sections.text: Sliding window = a rolling average, drop the oldest, add the newest. subtract leaving + add entering, never recompute the whole thing.

sections.title: Maximum Average Subarray I
sections.problemStatement: Given an array nums and an integer k, find the contiguous subarray of length k that has the maximum average value, and return that average.
hints: Maximizing the AVERAGE of a fixed-size window is the same as maximizing its SUM — the window size k never changes, so whichever window has the largest sum also has the largest average.
This is exactly this lesson's fixed-size max-subarray-sum example, with one extra step: divide the best sum by k at the end.
Build the first window's sum normally, then slide using subtract-leaving/add-entering, tracking the best sum seen.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Recompute each window's sum from scratch with a nested loop.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Fixed-size sliding window: build the first window's sum, then slide with subtract-leaving/add-entering, tracking the best sum.

sections.title: Minimum Size Subarray Sum
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of positive integers nums and a positive integer target, return the minimal length of a contiguous subarray whose sum is >= target. Return 0 if no such subarray exists.

examples.explanation: The subarray [4,3] has sum 7 and is the shortest.

hints: This is this lesson's previewed VARIABLE-size window — the window's size isn't fixed, it needs to grow and shrink as needed.
Expand the window's right edge, adding to the running sum, until the sum reaches at least target.
Once the sum is big enough, shrink from the LEFT as much as possible while the sum stays >= target, recording the smallest window length found along the way, then keep expanding right.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Check every possible subarray's sum with nested loops, tracking the shortest one meeting the target.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Variable-size sliding window: expand right to grow the sum, shrink left whenever the sum is already enough, tracking the minimum window length.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n) — each index enters and leaves the window at most once

sections.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
sections.problemStatement: Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.

examples.explanation: The longest substring without repeats is "abc".

hints: This is another variable-size window problem — 'longest substring' with a condition ('no repeats') that can be broken by growing too far.
You need fast membership checks for 'is this character already in my current window' — a hash set (recall Hashing) pairs naturally with the window.
Expand right, adding characters to the set. If the new character is already in the set, shrink from the left (removing characters from the set) until the duplicate is gone.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Check every substring for repeated characters, tracking the longest valid one found.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the seen set per check

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Variable-size sliding window with a hash set: expand right, shrink left on duplicates, tracking the max window size.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(min(n, charset size)) for the window set

sections.title: Permutation in String
sections.problemStatement: Given strings s1 and s2, return true if s2 contains a permutation (any character reordering) of s1 as a contiguous substring.
constraints: s1 and s2 lowercase English letters
hints: A permutation of s1 has EXACTLY the same character counts as s1, just reordered — you're really searching for any window with matching letter frequencies.
s1's length never changes, so this is a FIXED-size window (size = s1.length) sliding across s2, unlike the variable-size windows seen so far.
Track each window's letter counts as an array of 26 numbers, updating it with the same subtract-leaving/add-entering idea as any sliding window, and compare it to s1's target counts.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every window of s1's length in s2, sort both the window and s1 and compare — a sorted permutation looks identical.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Fixed-size sliding window using 26-letter frequency counts instead of sorting — slide by decrementing the leaving character's count and incrementing the entering one's.

sections.title: Find All Anagrams in a String
sections.problemStatement: Given strings s and p, return the starting indices of all of p's anagrams (character reorderings) found as substrings in s.

examples.explanation: "cba" at index 0 and "bac" at index 6 are both anagrams of "abc".

constraints: s and p lowercase English letters
hints: This is Permutation in String's exact technique — a fixed-size window (size = p.length) sliding across s, comparing letter-frequency counts.
The only difference: instead of returning true on the first match, COLLECT every starting index where the window's counts match p's.
Reuse the same 26-length frequency array and subtract-leaving/add-entering slide from Permutation in String.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every window of p's length in s, sort both and compare.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Fixed-size sliding window with 26-letter frequency counts, recording every index where the window matches p's counts.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) — fixed 26-size arrays, output not counted

sections.title: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
sections.problemStatement: Given a string s and an integer k, you may change up to k characters to any other uppercase letter. Return the length of the longest substring containing only one repeating letter after such changes.

examples.explanation: Replace both 'A's or both 'B's to get "AAAA" or "BBBB".

hints: A window is 'achievable' if you could turn it all into one letter using at most k replacements — that means (window length) - (count of its MOST frequent letter) <= k.
Track the count of the most frequent letter seen in the current window (maxFreq) as you expand right, using a 26-letter frequency array.

If a window ever becomes invalid (needs more than k replacements), shrink from the left by one — you don't need to recompute maxFreq exactly when shrinking, since the answer can only grow from a window at least as large as the best one already found.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Check every substring, counting its most frequent letter, to see if it's achievable within k replacements.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Sliding window tracking maxFreq as it grows; shrinks by one from the left whenever the window becomes invalid, without ever needing to shrink maxFreq back down.
